claim: Incidence rates of cervical cancer have increased over time.

result.claim: Incidence rates of cervical cancer have increased over time.
result.reasoning: Multiple sources indicate an increase in cervical cancer incidence rates over time. Paper ID 24671521, "Risk-adjusted cancer-incidence rates (United States)", reports an increase in the age-adjusted incidence-rate estimate of in situ and invasive cervical cancer from 57.8 to 66.3 per 100,000 person-years during 1990-92, and a significant increase for women aged 65 to 69 years. It also notes an increase in the lifetime risk of developing cervical cancer. Paper ID 3151695, "Incidence Trends of Adenocarcinoma of the Cervix in 13 European Countries", states that "Rapid increases in cervical adenocarcinoma incidence have been observed in Western countries in recent decades." Furthermore, Paper ID 667625, "Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection and intraepithelial neoplasia and invasive cancer of the uterine cervix: a case-control study in Zaragoza, Spain", mentions a "steady increase" in the incidence of uterine cervix cancer in Spain. Paper ID 78179198, "Knowledge of Cervical Cancer and its Associated Factors among Reproductive age Women at Robe and Goba Towns, Bale zone, Southeast Ethiopia", also highlights an "alarmingly" increasing incidence in Ethiopia. While some papers discuss decreases in mortality rates or incidence in specific regions due to screening, the overall evidence from various geographical locations and types of cervical cancer supports the claim of increasing incidence rates.

evidence.text: The number of women dying from cervical cancer in 1997 was 7% lower than in 1996.
evidence.source: Effect of screening on cervical cancer mortality in England and Wales: analysis of trends with an age period cohort model.

evidence.text: The age-adjusted incidence-rate estimate of in situ and invasive cervical cancer increased from 57.8 to 66.3 per 100,000 person-years during the period 1990-92.
evidence.source: Risk-adjusted cancer-incidence rates (United States)

evidence.text: The greatest increase in the incidence-rate estimate of in situ and invasive cervical cancer occurred for women aged 65 to 69 years, increasing from 40.1 to 63.2 per 100,000 person-years.
evidence.source: Risk-adjusted cancer-incidence rates (United States)

evidence.text: The aim of the study was to compare trends in incidence of and mortality from cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: The proportion of women who had undertaken cervical screening increased from 4.3% to 8.3% (p = 0.038).
evidence.source: Impact of health education intervention on knowledge and perception of cervical cancer and cervical screening uptake among adult women in rural communities in Nigeria

evidence.text: The raw incidence of cancer of the uterine cervix in Spain is 7.8 per 100,000 inhabitants.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection and intraepithelial neoplasia and invasive cancer of the uterine cervix: a case-control study in Zaragoza, Spain

evidence.text: Finland and the Netherlands have had a national screening program for cervical cancer for several years.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: Every year, 7095 women are diagnosed with cervical cancer in Ethiopia.
evidence.source: Knowledge of Cervical Cancer and its Associated Factors among Reproductive age Women at Robe and Goba Towns, Bale zone, Southeast Ethiopia

evidence.text: The adjusted incidence of cancer of the uterine cervix in Spain is 5.6 per 100,000 inhabitants.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection and intraepithelial neoplasia and invasive cancer of the uterine cervix: a case-control study in Zaragoza, Spain

evidence.text: In 1991, the cervical carcinoma mortality rate was 1.4 per 10(5) woman-years.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: Rapid increases in cervical adenocarcinoma incidence have been observed in Western countries in recent decades.
evidence.source: Incidence Trends of Adenocarcinoma of the Cervix in 13 European Countries

evidence.text: The mean incidence of cervical carcinoma in the early 1960s was 15.4 per 10(5) woman-years.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: In the early 1960s, the cervical carcinoma mortality rate was 6.6 per 10(5) woman-years.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: The number of women dying from cervical cancer has fallen by over 25% since 1992.
evidence.source: Effect of screening on cervical cancer mortality in England and Wales: analysis of trends with an age period cohort model.

evidence.text: Screening may have had at least some impact in reducing cervical adenocarcinoma incidence in several countries during the 1990s.
evidence.source: Incidence Trends of Adenocarcinoma of the Cervix in 13 European Countries

evidence.text: The proportion of women with very good knowledge of cervical cancer and screening rose from 2% to 70.5% (χ2 = 503.7, p < 0.0001).
evidence.source: Impact of health education intervention on knowledge and perception of cervical cancer and cervical screening uptake among adult women in rural communities in Nigeria

evidence.text: The proportion of women who had undertaken cervical screening increased from 4.3% to 8.3% (p = 0.038).
evidence.source: Impact of health education intervention on knowledge and perception of cervical cancer and cervical screening uptake among adult women in rural communities in Nigeria

evidence.text: The age-adjusted incidence-rate estimate for in situ and invasive cervical cancer increased from 57.8 to 66.3 per 100,000 person-years when applying the risk-adjusted incidence method for the period 1990-92.
evidence.source: Risk-adjusted cancer-incidence rates (United States)

evidence.text: The lifetime risk of developing cervical cancer increased from 4.6 percent (1 in 22) to 5.5 percent (1 in 18).
evidence.source: Risk-adjusted cancer-incidence rates (United States)

evidence.text: In 1991, the incidence of cervical carcinoma was 2.7 per 10(5) woman-years.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.
